# Shibari Atlas — dense source study guide

Retrieved: 2026-07-16

Primary source: https://shibariatlas.org/

This is an original, compressed study guide to the atlas's public facts, not a verbatim mirror. Structured facts are identified by the source as CC0 where possible; original atlas prose is CC BY-SA 4.0. Paraphrased portions derived from atlas prose are shared under CC BY-SA 4.0. Third-party links are evidence pointers only, and their content is not reproduced here.

The atlas is a curated, non-exhaustive public research view. Absence is not a judgment of importance, and current-person dates or legal names are intentionally omitted when they are not public.

## Project scope, terminology, and research method

### About

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/about/

Fact — 763 people 658 entries 3,173 connections 4,794 sources

- Linked evidence or record: [763 people](https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/); [658 entries](https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/); [3,173 connections](https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/); [4,794 sources](https://shibariatlas.org/sources/)
#### What this is

Fact — The project tracks how Japanese rope practices moved from historical restraint systems and twentieth-century publishing into contemporary international scenes. It is not a directory of everyone who ties rope. It is a curated dataset of people, schools, studios, dojos, bars, venues, events, publications, films and context entries with enough public evidence to explain a historical or structural role.

Transmission — The old working name was Bakushi Map. The public umbrella is now Shibari Atlas; the lineage section is now called Bakushi Lineage.

#### Three surfaces

Fact — One research base, three readings. Each surface answers a different question; entries and sources are shared.

##### Lineage

Fact — 3,173 documented connections

Fact — Time-centered genealogy view. Vertical position is time, columns group practitioners by school, and documented relationships show teaching, collaboration and publication ties.

Fact — Anchor figures

##### Map

Fact — 67 countries · 270 cities

Fact — 1213 located entries pinned to where they actually exist or happened: studios, dojos, bars, venues, events and resident practitioners on a navigable world map.

Fact — Dense cities

##### Events

Fact — 84 recurring events

Transmission — Calendar-year schedule of festivals, conventions, camps and performance dates, with each year keeping its own URL and linking occurrences back into the lineage.

Fact — Up next

#### Beta status

Fact — Shibari Atlas is in public beta. The interface is live and usable today, but the dataset behind it is still being expanded and corrected. Read it as a working draft that is actively maintained — not a finished or exhaustive reference.

- What already works: Transmission — All three surfaces are functional, and every entry is anchored to public references. The historical core — from the early pioneers to the major twentieth-century lineages — is the most thoroughly documented part of the dataset.
- What is still moving: Fact — Coverage is uneven. Some regions and many contemporary scenes are far thinner than the historical record. Entries are added in batches, and relationships are revised as stronger sources surface. A gap is expected; an absence is not a verdict.
- Deliberate gaps: Fact — Missing birth years are intentional when a practitioner has not published them, and legal names are avoided unless already part of someone's chosen public profile. These omissions are policy, not oversight.
- How you can help: Fact — Corrections, missing sources and boundary questions go to pussynawa@proton.me. The full editorial standard lives on the methodology page; the evidence list is at sources.
#### Data & licensing

Fact — Structured facts are released under CC0 where legally possible: IDs, names, public aliases, years, locations, entry categories and relationship categories. Original project prose is licensed under CC BY-SA 4.0.

Fact — Third-party sources remain under their own rights. Source links support verification and attribution; they do not import linked text into this project's license.

#### Frequently asked

Fact — New here? Start with the terms, then the project specifics. The editorial detail behind these answers lives on the methodology page.

- Linked evidence or record: [methodology](https://shibariatlas.org/methodology/)
##### What is bakushi?

Fact — Bakushi (縛師, lit. “tying master”) is the Japanese term for a rope artist — a practitioner of kinbaku. The word denotes a person; the practice itself is kinbaku or shibari. Traditionally the title was reserved for established rope masters working professionally in twentieth-century Japan; outside Japan it is used more loosely.

##### What is kinbaku?

Fact — Kinbaku (緊縛, lit. “tight binding”) is the Japanese art of erotic rope bondage. It descends from hojōjutsu, the Edo-period martial art of restraining prisoners with rope, and was reshaped via twentieth-century SM magazines, photography and performance into the form practised today.

##### What is the difference between shibari and kinbaku?

Fact — The two are often used interchangeably and the line between them is contested. In broad practice: shibari (縛り) literally means “tying” and is commonly used outside Japan as an umbrella term; kinbaku (緊縛) carries a stronger sense of the erotic, ritual and emotional tradition rooted in twentieth-century Japan.

##### What is the difference between bondage, shibari and kinbaku?

Fact — Bondage is the general Western BDSM term for consensual restraint by any means — rope, cuffs, tape, chains — with no tie to Japan or to rope specifically. Shibari and kinbaku (see above) are Japanese rope-specific terms, and usage genuinely splits: some treat them as a subset of bondage, some use all three interchangeably, and some reserve kinbaku for a fuller ritual/erotic practice against shibari as bare technique. A smaller strand of practitioners also questions non-Japanese use of the Japanese terms at all. None of these usages is settled — the atlas does not take a side.

##### What is hojōjutsu?

Fact — Hojōjutsu (捕縄術) is the Edo-period martial art of arresting and restraining prisoners with rope, developed for samurai and feudal-era police. Its formal patterns and rules about cord placement fed directly into the visual vocabulary of twentieth-century kinbaku — even though the latter is an erotic / artistic practice with no enforcement function.

##### What is the difference between nawashi, rigger, kinbakushi and bakushi?

Fact — Nawashi (縄師, lit. “rope master”), kinbakushi (緊縛師, lit. “tight-binding master”) and bakushi (縛師, lit. “tying master” — see above) are Japanese terms for a skilled rope practitioner, generally treated as overlapping — bakushi as a contraction of kinbakushi, nawashi as the form most familiar outside Japan — though some glossaries push finer, thinly sourced distinctions (nawashi toward craft, kinbakushi toward kinbaku’s erotic tradition) and describe the titles as earned via recognition rather than self-claimed. Rigger is a separate, generic Western BDSM term for whoever is tying — materials-agnostic and functional rather than honorific. Which label fits a given practitioner is a live, unresolved disagreement, not a settled convention.

##### What changed from “Bakushi Map” to “Shibari Atlas”?

Transmission — The project began as Bakushi Map. The public umbrella is now Shibari Atlas — broader than the genealogy alone — while the lineage section is now called Bakushi Lineage. Same dataset, same curator; only the framing widened to hold the map and events alongside the lineage.

##### How is the data sourced?

Fact — Every person, studio, bar or venue appears only when there is enough public evidence to anchor them meaningfully — typically Nawapedia entries, ShibariStudy listings, festival rosters, interview transcripts, archived venue pages and practitioners’ own published material. Direct apprenticeship requires explicit confirmation from at least one source. The public source list is at sources.

- Linked evidence or record: [sources](https://shibariatlas.org/sources/)
##### Why are some practitioners’ birth years missing?

Fact — Many bakushi do not publish their birth year, and the atlas respects that. A missing date is a deliberate gap, not an oversight. The same principle applies to legal names versus stage names — the atlas uses whatever the practitioner has publicly chosen to share.

##### What lineages and schools does it cover?

Transmission — Eleven named lineages plus an “independent” bucket: foundational (Seiu Ito and the early pioneers), Nureki, Akechi, Yukimura-ryū, Naka-ryū, Osada / Western, Kanna-ryū, Yagami-ryū, Kazami-ryū, Ichinawa-ryū and independent. Lineage is assigned by primary teacher / stated affiliation; cross-lineage practitioners are visible in their connections.

##### What's the difference between a studio, a dojo, a community, a bar and a venue in this atlas?

Transmission — These five categories are this atlas's own classification, not an industry standard, and the lines get fuzzy in practice. Dojo marks a lineage-anchoring school — a named teacher with formal ryū structure, or at least a primary teaching anchor; studio covers physical rope/teaching spaces without that kind of lineage anchor. Bar is used for fetish/SM/salon bars even when they host kinbaku classes, while venue covers broader kink/adult spaces — dungeons, private-members clubs — where rope is just one programming strand. Community applies to an ongoing social group or scene rather than a single event, studio, publication or platform.

##### Can I add, correct or remove an entry?

Fact — Yes — that is how the atlas grows. Use how to contribute for corrections, missing public references, privacy changes or removal requests.

- Linked evidence or record: [how to contribute](https://shibariatlas.org/about/#contribute)
##### Can I reuse the data or text?

Fact — Structured facts are released under CC0 where legally possible. Original project prose is CC BY-SA 4.0 and third-party sources keep their own rights. See data & licensing.

- Linked evidence or record: [data & licensing](https://shibariatlas.org/about/#data)
#### How to contribute

Fact — The atlas grows by correction. If you can point to a public source — or you are the person an entry characterizes — your input directly shapes what is shown. Here is what helps, and where it goes.

##### Fix or refine an entry

Transmission — A wrong bio line, a relationship that should be downgraded from apprenticeship to influence, a missing studio or year. Tell me what is off and what it should be.

##### Add a source

Fact — Found an official page, festival roster, interview or archive that anchors an entry or a connection? Send the link — strong public evidence is what moves a lead into the visible map.

##### Privacy & removal

Safety / consent — Want a name made less specific, two merged identities split, or an entry removed? Consent and safety take priority over a dense display — these are handled first.

Fact — What to include

- Fact — The entry or relationship which person, place, event or connection is affected
- Fact — What should change the specific fix, in your own words
- Transmission — A public source URL where possible — preferred for lineage, rank or contested claims
- Safety / consent — Mark private requests clearly for access, identity, location, legal-name, safety or removal requests
Fact — Send it to

Fact — For ordinary public-source corrections, you can also reach out on @shibari.atlas. The full editorial standard lives on the methodology page.

- Linked evidence or record: [@shibari.atlas](https://www.instagram.com/shibari.atlas/); [methodology](https://shibariatlas.org/methodology/)
Fact — If you are the person an entry characterizes, say so in the message. A public source is preferred but not always required — state clearly what may be published.

### Methodology

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/methodology/

#### 01 What the lineage view shows

Fact — The page presents a public research view of shibari / kinbaku people, schools, studios, rope bars, venues, festivals, publications, platforms, material infrastructure and historical context. It is not a complete directory, a ranking, a certification registry, or a claim that every important person is visible.

Fact — Entries are added when they help explain transmission, scene infrastructure or historical context:

- Transmission — People with public rope identities — teachers, performers, organizers, authors, photographers, riggers, models, rope makers or lineage carriers — when that identity also has a graph-safe relationship or a clearly structural / historical role
- Fact — Recurring studios, dojos, festivals, conventions, communities and education platforms
- Fact — Publications, films, production labels and material projects when they shaped rope culture or connect multiple existing entries
- Fact — Historical or regional context when it explains why clusters moved or changed
Transmission — The view is intentionally selective. A person or event may be important locally and still stay out of the visible lineage if the public references are too thin, if inclusion would expose private information, or if the public evidence does not yet explain a wider historical or structural role. A well-sourced current-person profile is not enough by itself; without a reference-supported relationship, infrastructure role or historical bridge, it stays source-only / watch rather than becoming an isolated visible node.

#### 02 Evidence levels

Fact — The project separates evidence into three practical levels. What a source can support depends on which level it sits at.

##### Strong

Fact — Official bios, studio pages, event rosters, publisher pages, interviews, archived festival pages, books, public talks, or direct pages maintained by the person or organization. Supports entries, biography facts, source links and visible relationships.

##### Medium

Fact — Public social posts, directory rows, event mirrors, podcast pages, third-party writeups, or archived traces. Supports limited role, event or link context when it corroborates an already public identity.

##### Weak

Fact — Unsourced records, reposts, dead pages without archive context, search snippets, anonymous claims, private screenshots, memory or rumor. Used as a research lead, not as a public claim.

Fact — If two sources conflict, the project prefers the more direct source: the person's own current public statement, then official studio / event records, then interviews and specialist archives, then broader secondary sources. Older sources are kept only when they explain historical context and do not conflict with a subsequent correction.

#### 03 Source handling

Fact — Preferred sources are official pages, event rosters, studio biographies, interviews, archived festival pages, publisher pages, public talks and stable community documentation. Directory rows and social posts are used as leads, not as enough evidence on their own, unless they corroborate an already public role.

Fact — Each visible entry should have at least one public link, unless the entry is broad historical context supported by the public source list. Repeated sources are kept in a shared registry so readers can check the evidence without the same link being repeated in every paragraph.

Fact — Source links support verification and attribution. They do not mean the linked source endorses Shibari Atlas, and they do not import the linked text into the project's license. For visible relationships, source records must support the relationship itself — a source that only proves two entries exist, or only places them in the same broad scene, is not enough.

#### 04 Documented corpus profile

Fact — These counts describe the public material currently documented by Shibari Atlas. They do not measure the size, importance or geographic distribution of the underlying rope scene.

Fact — The profile is a release snapshot, not a score. A high count can reflect accessible archives, language access or concentrated research effort; a low count can reflect missing metadata or documentation gaps. Unknown values stay visible instead of being inferred.

- Source records: Fact — 4,794
- Cited entries: Fact — 1,421
- Source-to-entry citations: Fact — 6,719

Fact — Type and language use source records as their denominator. Era and country use unique source-to-entry citations, so one source cited by two entries contributes twice. Duplicate citations on one entry contribute once. Only citations attached to people and organizations participate; citations attached to individual relationships, works or programme sections remain separate.

Fact — Open the public references catalogue to inspect individual public references. The catalogue keeps its own pagination and search; this profile publishes aggregates only.

- Linked evidence or record: [Open the public Sources catalogue](https://shibariatlas.org/sources/)
#### 05 From claim to visible map

Fact — Not every true or interesting fact becomes a visible relationship. Every claim lands at the strictest level its public evidence supports — most never reach the map:

##### Not used

Fact — An attractive inference that is misleading, unsafe, duplicate or unsupported.

##### Research lead

Fact — Worth revisiting later, but not presented publicly yet.

##### Biography context

Fact — Useful for understanding an entry but too narrow, private, ambiguous or weak to draw.

##### Visible relationship

Transmission — A source directly supports a specific connection that explains transmission, collaboration, publication, teaching or lineage.

##### Visible entry

Fact — Explains several relationships or a structurally important bridge.

Fact — The project avoids hub inflation. A magazine, festival, podcast, adult-media studio, social platform or directory is not connected to everyone it mentions. Relationships are shown only when meaningful for the lineage view — not merely because two names appear on the same page.

Fact — The project also avoids person-node inflation. Public social profiles, link hubs, interviews, directories and photo credits can be valuable source context, but if they do not support a graph-safe relationship or structural role, they remain source-only / watch. The map should not contain weak standalone current-person nodes simply because those people have real public rope identities.

#### 06 Era placement

Fact — Each visible entry is assigned to an era as a start-era label — the earliest reference-supported public period in which that subject becomes relevant to rope history in the form shown on the map.

- Fact — 1800s
- Fact — Edo / Meiji
- Fact — 1900s
- Fact — Taishō foundations
- Fact — 1950s
- Fact — Magazine era
- Fact — 1970s
- Fact — SM revolution
- Fact — 1980s
- Fact — Schools form
- Fact — 1990s
- Fact — Second generation
- Fact — 2000s
- Fact — Global diffusion
- Fact — 2010s
- Fact — Networked pedagogy
- Fact — 2020s
- Fact — Distributed infrastructure
- Fact — A person is placed by the first documented public rope role — publication, performance, teaching, organizing, named studio role, or explicit entry into the public scene.
- Fact — A studio, bar, venue, festival, platform or publication is placed by its first documented opening, founding, edition, issue or release.
- Fact — The project does not place entries by birth year, by current fame, or by whatever decade they are most active in today.
- Transmission — Later activity does not move an older entry into a newer era. If a senior practitioner later studies with a younger teacher, that is recorded as subsequent cross-era study; their era still reflects their own public starting period.
Fact — When one continuous node would collapse distinct historical phases into a misleading single band, the project splits that subject into separate era entries instead of dragging the whole history into the present.

#### 07 Relationship categories

Fact — The view distinguishes relationship strength with a small set of public categories. Each has a categorically different line so it survives even when the map repaints edges by school colour.

##### Deshi

Transmission — Explicit formal apprenticeship, named successor, or sustained primary-teacher lineage. The only solid line.

##### Certification

Fact — Instructor license, school authorization or formal rank that is public — but not framed as apprenticeship. Shown as a double rail.

##### Influence

Transmission — Workshop study, private lessons, named stylistic influence or non-formal learning. The lightest tie.

##### Founded / operates

Fact — Established, owns, operates, hosts or anchors a structured school, studio, platform, event or media line.

##### Appeared / credited

Fact — Verified presenter, workshop, festival, stage, magazine, film, production or other credited appearance.

##### Collaboration

Fact — Co-created, co-organized, partner project, staff/editor role or durable rope partnership framed as real working ties.

Fact — Workshop attendance alone is not treated as formal apprenticeship. Event co-presence never creates a person-to-person collaboration edge by itself. To avoid hub inflation, broad teacher records fan out only to teachers who already have a node or are independently graph-worthy.

##### How Trace Connection chooses a path

Transmission — Transmission uses only person-to-person apprenticeship, certification and explicit study / style / mixed influence. All documented connections can also use collaborations, studios, events, media and context. The route is calculated from the complete public graph, so saved visual filters never change a shared trace URL.

Fact — The algorithm prefers direct transmission semantics and adds a kind-and-degree penalty to intermediate entity hubs such as festivals or broad context entries. Equal routes prefer fewer steps and then immutable relationship IDs. Source counts, confidence and editorial assessments are not route weights. Every step keeps its recorded direction, traversal direction, public role / year / note and evidence; absent facts are shown as not recorded rather than inferred.

#### 08 Living people & privacy

Fact — For living or contemporary people, the project uses public scene names by default. Birth years, legal names, private residences and adult-media aliases are not added unless the person uses them in a stable public professional context and the information is necessary for the public presentation.

Fact — Missing birth years are expected. They are not a data-quality failure. Locations are public scene anchors — a studio city, festival city, teaching base or country-level scene context — not private addresses.

Safety / consent — If a living person asks for a correction, removal, identity split, privacy downgrade or safer wording, that request is handled as a high-priority signal. Public safety and consent matter more than preserving a visually dense display.

#### 09 Queer, gender & sexuality context

Fact — Queer, trans, nonbinary, women / femininities or marginalized-community context may be included when it is part of the public rope-facing identity, event scope, community mission, pedagogy or historical role being documented.

Fact — For people, use only self-description, a direct public source maintained by the person or their project, or an official event / studio / publisher bio. Keep the wording close to the source. Do not broaden a specific public statement into a larger identity label, and do not infer identity from participation in a queer event, partnerships, appearance or scene reputation.

Fact — The graph keeps no dedicated identity field or "representatives" taxonomy. Identity context belongs in biography / source context, and should never be used to rank, tokenize, out, deanonymize or expose sensitive identity beyond the source's own public framing.

#### 10 Self-reported information

Fact — Direct corrections or additions from the person an entry characterizes may be used when they clearly allow publication — by email, direct message, public comment or another first-person channel. Self-reported information is treated as first-party correction material, not as a public source; private emails, messages and screenshots are not published in the source list.

Transmission — Allowed self-reported updates include preferred name, spelling, pronouns, role, location scope, public links or removal requests; corrections to biography, teacher framing, partner context, studio affiliation or event framing; identity splits where two public names were incorrectly merged; and privacy downgrades. For strong lineage, rank or controversial claims, a public source is preferred.

#### 11 Adult-media & sensitive sources

Fact — Adult-media sources are handled narrowly. They are included only when they support a rope-specific public role: rigger, bondage director, rope consultant, production rope educator, selected rope-video work, or a production project that materially shaped rope visual culture.

Fact — The project does not import filmographies, one-off performer credits, scraped adult databases, ambiguous aliases or links that expose private identity beyond a public rope role. Sensitive sources should never be used to out, deanonymize, shame, rank or sensationalize a person. If a fact is not needed to explain rope transmission or infrastructure, it stays out.

#### 12 Licensing & attribution

Fact — The public dataset is split by content type. Its structured facts can be released under CC0 where legally possible: public entry identifiers, names, aliases, dates, scene locations, entry categories and relationship categories. Any public export must exclude biographies and other expressive prose, contact and reference URLs, canonical source identifiers, source-registry metadata and internal editorial fields. Where a fact has public evidence, an export may carry only an opaque citation key that can be resolved via this site's public citation layer.

Fact — Third-party source material remains under its original license. Biographies and notes should be independently written factual summaries — the project does not intentionally import verbatim third-party prose or close translations. If copied prose is found, it is rewritten, removed, or attributed and licensed according to the source.

#### 13 Editorial checks

Fact — Each update is checked against the same editorial principles:

- Fact — Public links or source references for updated entries and relationships
- Fact — Conservative relationship categories with short explanatory notes
- Fact — Weak leads held back until better public evidence appears
- Fact — Well-sourced but isolated current-person leads kept source-only instead of becoming weak standalone nodes
- Fact — Tempting but unsupported inferences left out
- Fact — Consistency checks for relationship categories, dates, locations and public links
Fact — Known uncertainty is preferable to false precision. The project should say less when the sources say less.

#### 14 Corrections

Fact — If the correction comes directly from the person an entry characterizes, a public source is preferred but not always required; the person should state explicitly what may be published. Preferred outcomes are precise: fix a bio sentence, downgrade apprenticeship to influence, remove an unsupported relationship, add a source link, split two merged identities, or hide a private detail.

Fact — A correction, removal, access or privacy request about a record describing you can be sent via the contribution contact. Mark that the request concerns you and state what may be published. The message is reviewed manually; it is not published as a source and the project does not promise a fixed outcome or response period.

Fact — Help build the atlas

Fact — Corrections, missing sources and requests about a record describing you use the contribution guide.


### Updates

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/updates/

#### Format

Fact — The page presents a project changelog, not a news feed — it covers changes to this dataset and this interface, not the wider shibari/kinbaku scene. Each entry groups a period of work into three kinds of change: Dataset (people, entities and connections added or corrected), Research (naming, lineage or classification calls revised), and Interface (features and fixes to the site itself).

Fact — Each entry groups completed changes by outcome and reflects the public atlas at the end of the week, omitting temporary work that did not remain part of the release. For the editorial rules behind the dataset, see methodology; for the public references behind it, see sources.

- Linked evidence or record: [methodology](https://shibariatlas.org/methodology/); [sources](https://shibariatlas.org/sources/)
#### Log

##### Evidence-aware profiles and the 0.2 release checkpoint

Transmission — Closed the 0.2 release checkpoint around exact evidence, resilient delivery and mobile Lineage behavior, while extending the historical event archive and regional atlas coverage.

- Transmission — Research Expanded exact claim-level provenance for selected life dates, active periods, institutional histories and formal lineage fields, with structured source limitations and regression checks guarding citation metadata.
- Transmission — Interface Connected reviewed citations to selected profile facts and relationship evidence, added readable static Lineage and profile fallbacks for missing JavaScript or bundles, and simplified profile, citation and correction controls.
- Transmission — Interface Shipped a mobile-first Lineage directory that opens profiles in Hyper Focus, then tightened camera fitting, touch behavior, edge routing, deep-link centering and cross-surface links between Lineage, Map and Events.
- Transmission — Interface Added production-preview smoke checks, a Node compatibility lane, retained browser failure artifacts, bounded Lineage and Map delivery to 128 shards, reduced Sources page size and introduced release-blocking capacity budgets.
- Fact — Dataset Extended the event archive spanning 2004–2023 and added or enriched people, studios and communities such as Kokoro Kinbaku, Fittglitter, Turo Shibari and Shibariland, Kanso, Van Darkholme, Lochai, Rope Jam Studio, Shibari Muses, Shibari Study Valencia and Entwine NV.
##### Festival archive and a steadier Lineage

Fact — Expanded the historical events ledger and archive-roster links while hardening Hyper Focus for iPhone memory limits, touch navigation and dense relationship views.

- Fact — Dataset Backfilled occurrences spanning BoundCon, EURIX, RopeFest, LFRA, Onawa and Omuchi Asobi, Rope Spirit, Helsinki Shibari, Bondage Expo Dallas, Shi Fest Ukraine, Rome Bondage Week, La Nuit des Cordes, BOUND London, Bound In Boston, ROPECRAFT, Prague Shibari Festival, MoscowKnot and RopeDay.
- Fact — Research Connected verified archive participants to existing profiles spanning ROPECRAFT, Helsinki, Shi Fest, Rope Spirit, BOUND London, EURIX, Onawa and Omuchi, Prague and Rome, without turning simple co-presence into lineage claims.
- Transmission — Interface Replaced oversized Lineage canvas layers with viewport-sized iPhone overlays, released hidden-tab canvas memory, improved dense note-label routing and edge highlighting, and tightened mobile Hyper Focus selection and side-panel behavior.
- Fact — Dataset Applied the Kinbaku Today article-family audit and broadened reference-supported coverage via additions such as the Rope Podcast hosts, Knots N’Roots Costa Rica, Triangle Shibari, Tüdeltreff Kiel and Dov / Washi Nawashi.
##### Hyper Focus and a broader contextual atlas

Transmission — Introduced a shareable one-hop Lineage view, reorganized recent-era and regional context layers, and expanded connected profiles and public contact coverage via focused source audits.

- Transmission — Interface Added Hyper Focus as a shareable one-hop Lineage view with category lanes, era rows, always-visible connection notes, mobile access, touch-friendly controls and curated first-visit framing.
- Fact — Dataset Split recent historical context into clearer 2010s and 2020s layers, added explicit regional scene sublayers and era spans, and surfaced those spans on selected context profiles.
- Fact — Dataset Expanded connected coverage spanning Japanese studios and practitioners, festival performers and regional communities, such as Maitake, Toronawa / MilkyWay, Enbushinbakukai, Hanikamu Nawakai, Enchou Studio, Why Knot Lille and Thailand BDSM Community.
- Fact — Research Completed broad public-contact and source-family passes spanning Bluesky, Instagram, Shibarimap, books and Kinbaku Today, while leaving weak or unstable leads out until stronger evidence appears.
##### Faster Lineage delivery and a global infrastructure pass

Transmission — Made the Lineage and Map lighter, faster and more accessible, expanded local-scene and event coverage worldwide, and completed a systematic reconciliation of relationships, entity types and source links.

- Transmission — Interface Moved the Lineage graph into a cached static payload and made biographies load only when opened, dramatically shrinking profile-page HTML; also sped up zooming and path highlighting, reduced mobile edge rendering and fixed narrow-screen edge clipping and bottom-sheet gestures.
- Fact — Interface Reduced repeated work spanning the Map, minimap and home search, added keyboard and screen-reader access with visible focus treatment, improved event-page readability and corrected Event and Lineage structured-data warnings.
- Fact — Dataset Added people, studios, communities and venues spanning Perth, Singapore, Montenegro, Latin America, Cape Town, continental Europe and North America, alongside new occurrences for Alive Kinbaku Festival, Men Roping Men, BoFeWo and Kinbaku Dreams.
- Transmission — Research Reviewed sparse relationship records, major event and studio hubs, era placement and entity classification; synchronized verified event appearances with the Lineage and introduced clearer venue and event subtypes.
- Fact — Research Reconciled duplicate studios, shared URLs and project-versus-person link scope spanning a broad source pass, correcting many ambiguous profile, team, event and institutional references without inflating the graph.
##### A new map and connected reference pages

Fact — Rebuilt the geographic atlas and its public reference pages as clearer, searchable surfaces, while expanding global profiles, event coverage and historical source context.

- Fact — Interface Redesigned the Map around a dark labelled basemap, category-coloured pins and clusters, a collapsible country-grouped index and a draggable mobile bottom sheet; it now defaults to living, currently active people and places and groups country-level records under shared pins.
- Fact — Interface Rebuilt About, Methodology and Sources as a connected reference set with live dataset counts, evidence-level explainers, an anchored FAQ and a searchable, filterable source index that exposes previously hidden catalog and database records.
- Fact — Interface Added dedicated pages for individual event occurrences with structured event data, introduced a distinct visual treatment for certification relationships, and fixed antimeridian centering, popup auto-pan and untimed programme-row layout.
- Fact — Dataset Expanded people, studios, communities and event links spanning Japan, Europe, Asia-Pacific and Latin America, such as new programme and roster context for Shibari Camp Poland, Prague Autumn, QuIRKS, Kinky Breizh Ropecamp and Querverbindungen.
- Fact — Research Deepened historical provenance via focused Nawa-art, Kinbakunomicon, Kitan Club and related media-source audits, and separated instructor certification from broader teaching and influence relationships.
##### Timeline Ledger and global scene coverage

Fact — Rebuilt annual event browsing into a clearer Timeline Ledger, expanded profiles and rosters spanning Japan, Europe and the wider global scene, and strengthened the atlas's public status and evidence workflow.

- Fact — Interface Redesigned yearly Events as a month-by-month Timeline Ledger with a current-date divider, status badges, compact rosters, linked profile names and clear event-site and Lineage actions.
- Fact — Interface Added a site-wide Beta Status badge, contact blocks for studios and events, bestowed-name profile metadata and structured-data fixes for About, Methodology and Sources.
- Fact — Dataset Expanded coverage spanning Latin America, South Africa, the Baltics, Belfast, Hong Kong, New Zealand, India and Japan, such as an extensive Ichinawakai instructor pass and new studio and community clusters.
- Fact — Research Filled 2026 schedule and roster gaps for Bogotá, EURIX, Prague, Pinse, BoundCon, Shibari Camp Poland, Danish Shibari Festival, Queerope and Onawa Asobi Europe, and added Kinbaku Yuga, RopeFest and International Shibari Film Festival coverage.
- Transmission — Research Simplified relationship types while separating appearances and editorial context, then added a generated source ledger and event-lineage cross-check to keep evidence and event-derived links auditable.
##### Post-launch expansion and relationship provenance

Transmission — Expanded documented people, places and events spanning several regions, completed a major relationship-provenance pass, and made atlas navigation and Lineage reading more precise after launch.

- Fact — Dataset Expanded the atlas with new clusters spanning Japan, China, Korea, the Balkans, Central Europe, Canada, Australia and Latin America, alongside selected studios, dojos, festivals and documentary-film entries.
- Transmission — Research Completed a source pass spanning performance, collaboration, dojo, operator and study or influence relationships, adding role and evidence metadata, source references, chronology checks and explicit influence kinds.
- Fact — Research Audited Nawapedia's kinbakushi and model categories, a Japanese historical chronology and several podcast and source families, promoting well-supported profiles while keeping weak leads out of the atlas.
- Fact — Interface Added a Places catalogue and direct city or country map focus, event-occurrence deep links, accent-insensitive search and explicit profile contact links.
- Fact — Interface Improved mobile selection framing and zoom text sharpness, marked recently added profiles, and clarified parallel relationship paths, labels and endpoints.
##### Shibari Atlas public beta launch

Chronology — After a week of launch preparation, the project — until then called Bakushi Map — relaunched publicly as Shibari Atlas for the first time: a live site on its own domain with search-engine visibility, mobile support, and a published data methodology behind it.

- Transmission — Interface Renamed the project from Bakushi Map to Shibari Atlas, a broader public umbrella covering the map and events alongside the lineage, and opened it to the public at bakushimap.org, with per-entity profile pages, a sitemap, a custom 404 page, and social-preview images generated from the live map; added an About overlay that opens automatically on first visit alongside dedicated Methodology and Sources pages explaining the project's inclusion criteria and sourcing standards; and built out mobile support with pinch-zoom, touch panning, and a collapsible filter bar with a bottom-sheet detail panel.
- Fact — Dataset Substantially expanded regional coverage — Russian, Ukrainian, Belarusian, Polish and other national scenes, such as diaspora connections following the war in Ukraine — and added many new festival and event nodes worldwide, plus new studios, podcasts and community platforms as tracked sources, alongside a new authored-works category linking writers to their published books.
- Fact — Research Published a public data methodology covering inclusion criteria, source standards, and a narrow policy for adult-media sources tied to a specific rope-related professional role; reclassified roughly 40 entries from a generic "venue" type into distinct event and studio categories and gave adult-media production companies their own category separate from teaching studios; and established a policy for handling self-reported corrections from people who appear in the dataset.

## People, institutions, works, materials, and historical contexts

### ARCADIA Group

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/bars/arcadia-group/

Fact — ARCADIA Group, 2003–, is documented as a Japanese bar in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 2003–

Fact — Era: 2000s

Fact — Nagare Aotsuki's ARCADIA venue network and kinbaku / SM-scene infrastructure. The official Osaka site traces the group from Alcatraz SM Circle in 2000 to ARCADIA Osaka in 2003, ARCADIA Tokyo in 2009 and Titty Twister in 2016. Nawapedia and Kinbaku Today add MAJESTIC SM Bar as historical Nagare context, while ARCADIA Tokyo / Tesshin Doyama sources place Doyama as Tokyo general manager in 2009 and later owner / organizer of the Kabukicho venue and Bakkushinkai rope group.

- [ARCADIA Tokyo](http://www.arcadiatokyo.com/)
- [ARCADIA Tokyo](https://nawapedia.net/index.php?title=ARCADIA_Tokyo)
- [Titty Twister](https://www.tittytwister.jp/)

### ARCADIA Osaka

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/bars/arcadia-osaka/

Fact — ARCADIA Osaka, 2003–, is documented as a Japanese bar in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 2003–

Fact — Era: 2000s

Fact — Osaka SM Bar ARCADIA, the original ARCADIA venue opened by Nagare Aotsuki in 2003 after Alcatraz SM Circle. The first-party site characterizes it as an adult social space for SM, kinbaku, bondage and related minority interests in Umeda / Doyamacho, and records ARCADIA Tokyo and Titty Twister as ARCADIA Group venues.

### ARCADIA Tokyo

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/bars/arcadia-tokyo/

Fact — ARCADIA Tokyo, 2009–, is documented as a Japanese bar in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 2009–

Fact — Era: 2000s

Fact — Kabukicho / Shinjuku SM & Fetish bar in the ARCADIA Group. Nawapedia characterizes ARCADIA Tokyo as owned by Nagare Aotsuki and run by Tesshin Doyama, while Kinbaku Today says the Tokyo location opened when Arcadia expanded from Osaka to Tokyo in 2009 and that Doyama moved from Osaka to manage the new space. The first-party site gives the current venue and Bakkushinkai context.

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/bars/bar-bara-kyoto/

Fact — BAR-BARA Kyoto, 1995–, is documented as a Japanese bar in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 1995–

Fact — Era: 1990s

Fact — Kyoto salon-style SM / fetish bar and kinbaku scene location run by Ryuzaki Asuka. Kinbaku Today's Asuka interview traces the project from VELVET in November 1995 into BAR-BARA, with Nagoya branches in the late 1990s and the Kyoto salon continuing for decades. The official English page characterizes Asuka Ryuzaki welcoming visitors to BAR-BARA the Bizarre, a salon-style bar around fetishism, SM, bondage, shibari / kinbaku, tattoo and body piercing.

### Black Heart

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/bars/black-heart/

Fact — Black Heart, 1998–, is documented as a Japanese bar in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 1998–

Fact — Era: 1990s

Fact — Ginza fetish / SM salon opened by Tatsumi Naito in the late 1990s and used as a kinbaku classroom and performance / salon venue. SMpedia's Black Heart page records the venue's 1998 opening, its kinbaku classes from the early 2000s and the teacher sequence from Matsui Kenji to Akechi Denki and then Saikatsu. Kinbaku Today independently repeats that Black Heart was the Ginza club where Matsui first taught, Akechi took over in 2004 and Saikatsu became the third teacher. Metropolis characterizes Black Heart as an underground Ginza salon with weekly kinbaku workshops, and the current official site still frames it as a long-running Ginza salon bar for SM, fetish and kinbaku interests.

### Nakano Queen

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/bars/nakano-queen/

Fact — Nakano Queen, 1975–1991, is documented as a Japanese bar in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 1975–1991

Fact — Era: 1970s

Fact — Tokyo / Nakano SM club and salon tied to Seki Kazuko's late-Showa / early-Heisei work. Nawapedia places Seki at Nakano Queen from the mid-1970s, records the 1982 SM Classroom - Shibari Edition video at the venue, notes a Shin Kitan feature titled `Mama Kazuko - Nakano Queen`, and characterizes later exchange with Holland's Club Doma and S&M Sniper / SM Spirit publication context.

### Samm Pub / SAMM

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/bars/samm-pub/

Fact — Samm Pub / SAMM, 1981–2001, is documented as a Japanese bar in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 1981–2001

Fact — Era: 1980s

Fact — Roppongi high-membership SM salon / club launched in 1981 by Kayama Shigeru. Osada Steve's official Osada Eikichi chronology says Eikichi began appearing there in 1983 and characterizes Samm Pub as a high-class, high-priced SM club in Roppongi run by Kayama; the same page says the venue was the precursor to the Samm Video / HMP production line that closed in 2001. Nawapedia places Hiromi Saotome in the SAMM orbit from 1980, making SAMM a compact connection between Showa salon culture, Eikichi Osada's performance circuit and later SM-video infrastructure.

### ArtCo

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/communities/artco-russia/

Fact — ArtCo, 2010s–, is a Russian community in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 2010s–

Fact — Era: 2010s

Fact — Russian shibari / bondage creative union created by Boris Mosafir as an education and event-organizing umbrella. RopeFest Petersburg's ArtCo explainer says ArtCo runs teaching, festivals and shibari evenings in Moscow and Saint Petersburg via four formats: RopeFest in Moscow and Saint Petersburg, Bondage Cafe in Saint Petersburg, RopeDay in Moscow and RopeSchool education. The same source names Karol Soulrope as responsible for the Saint Petersburg RopeFest and Bondage Cafe, with Moscow RopeFest handled by Master Set. Nawapedia.ru names Art Co as the creator and maintainer of that Russian shibari wiki.

Fact — ASKA, 2010s–, is a French community in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 2010s–

Fact — Era: 2010s

Fact — Association Shibari Kinbaku Arts, a Toulouse-area association for shibari / kinbaku practice, courses, jams, stages, meetings, exhibitions and performances. HelloAsso characterizes initiations, all-level classes and jams twice a month around Labège; Stella Spei's official bio names her as ASKA president since 2019 and a Rope Camp organizer. Current ASKA traces include the recurring Shibari Mon Amour performance / exhibition evening and a 2026 Albi immersion masterclass with Kasumi Hourai.

Fact — Era: 2020s

The first-party site says EDO aims to inform, train and gather people around shibari, with activity around Nantes, Quimper, Brest and Vannes. Its public program language covers initiations, all-level classes, performances and free-practice rope jams, with a safety / respect / tolerance framing. EDO's partners page also records a small French association network such as Lyon Shibari, Port d'Attaches, Coven Cordes, Les Cordes Roses, Il Pleut des Cordes and Art des Cordes du Sud.

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/communities/austin-rope-slingers/

Fact — Austin Rope Slingers, 2015–, is documented as an American community in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 2015–

Fact — Era: 2010s

Fact — Austin, Texas volunteer-run peer rope group that began in spring 2015 as a weekly gathering for rope practice, knowledge sharing and education. The first-party site characterizes weekly Monday rope labs, 101 classes, higher-level peer education and intensives, rope-focused shindigs, munches, loaner rope, safety shears and negotiation resources. ROPECRAFT names Jake Wing as a current leader of Austin Rope Slingers, and ARS class pages show Jake teaching beginner and pandemic Zoom-lab rope classes.

Fact — Active: 2024–

Fact — Era: 2020s

Fact — Bangkok shibari training / awareness community also listed publicly as ShibariWorkshopsTH. Kinky Bangkok characterizes its mission as improving shibari awareness and facilitating training for people who want to learn or improve, spanning kink play, artistic expression and performance. Its Shibari Foundations course is structured as four self-contained workshops for riggers and bottoms, covering awareness, technique, connection and spontaneity; Laodai is named as the technique trainer.

Fact — Bondage Chile, 2004-, is a Chilean community in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 2004-

Fact — Era: 2000s

Fact — Santiago BDSM / shibari education community and project active since 2004. Doble Espacio's workshop report names Christian Leiva as creator of BondageChile.cl and characterizes the project as a Santiago workshop / dissemination shell.

### Bondage Soup

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/communities/bondage-soup/

Fact — Bondage Soup, 2018–, is documented as an American community in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 2018–

Fact — Era: 2010s

Fact — Portland-centered rope-education community / studio infrastructure operating via ROpenSpace PDX, with Bondage Soup as the core 12-month immersive curriculum for tying pairs. The official Bondage Soup pages describe a progressive monthly program blending foundational rope, suspensions, theory, negotiation and community practice, hosted via ROpenSpace with volunteer instructors, graduate assistants and alumni support. Shibari Study's studio directory now records ROpenSpace PDX as the public studio identity for the same Portland infrastructure; the atlas keeps one merged entry rather than splitting venue and curriculum until stronger separate public public roles appear. Current official program copy says the curriculum has evolved continuously since 2018 and now draws on feedback from nearly 150 graduates; the site also frames `Pressure Cooker` as a faster advanced offshoot for experienced tying pairs.

### Borderline Shibari

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/communities/borderline-shibari/

Fact — Borderline Shibari, 2017–, is a British community in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 2017–

Fact — Era: 2010s

Fact — Shibari education and community project established by Hua Hua in Shanghai in 2017, where she ran the first public bilingual shibari events in China. Grew into an international online/offline community after she relocated to London in 2022. Mission: share Shibari culture and provide high-quality educational content that is approachable for all. Programming includes private classes, online community via Patreon, and original shibari films. The name reflects rope as a connection spanning societal borders.

Fact — Active: 2020s–

Fact — Buenos Aires shibari learning and practice community coordinated by Chivy Brites. Club de Cuerdas programming includes classes, guided practice, themed meetings and shared rope events for riggers, models, switches and newcomers without partners. ### Clwb Nawa

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/communities/clwb-nawa/

Fact — Clwb Nawa, 2024–, is a British community in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 2024–

Fact — Era: 2020s

Fact — Peer-led kinbaku community in Cardiff, Wales, established by Oz and co-hosted with Red / The Siren at The Nest. Clwb Nawa runs intentionally small practice evenings centered on collaborative learning, focused technique, conversation and connection rather than formal instruction. A fundamentals pathway introduces essential skills before wider practice, while occasional guest workshops and demonstrations complement the regular peer programme.

Fact — Cordespace, 2014–, is a Canadian community in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 2014–

Fact — Era: 2010s

Fact — Quebec City shibari / kinbaku community active since 2014. Its official site characterizes educational and playful activities for learning Japanese-inspired rope in Quebec City, with regular classes, workshops, private coaching, practice evenings, social events, rope supplies and community resources around consent, communication, safety, self-awareness and progressive learning. The official team page records Ebi McKnotty as manager, teacher and supervision lead, making Cordespace the current offline community counterpart to her Rope365 curriculum work.

Fact — Active: 2012–

Fact — Era: 2010s

Fact — Los Angeles-centered anarchist rope collective started in 2012, focused on kinbaku / shibari education, primary-source and first-hand instruction, local Cell classes, rope jams and public events. Shibari Study's Oji Pan episode frames it as a deliberately leaderless collective around access to rope knowledge. The first-party site says members and friends now span beyond Los Angeles and that Ordinals, Ojipan and GoodMostTimes launched Devil Mask Studio in January 2024.

Fact — Active: 2020s–

Fact — Santa Cruz queer rope-education, performance and event-production project by Devon and Cass. Shibari Study frames Dual Demons as non-binary trauma-informed kink educators and performers specializing in rope suspension and queer anarchist spaces. The 418 Project characterizes them as teaching shibari rope bondage, performing immersive rope suspension, producing queer variety shows and producing K!nk at Pride in Santa Cruz. Their official events page records recurring rope labs, queer social hours and Bael Series shibari curriculum events at The 418 Project, and Twisted Windows records Dual Demons in its Bay Area rope-performance troupe.

Fact — Embodiment Rope Space, 2021–, is a community in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 2021–

Fact — Era: 2020s

Fact — Cape Town queer-first rope and shibari community space active since 2021. Official Embodiment pages describe a volunteer-run physical rope space in Salt River with weekly rope jams, beginner and intermediate workshops, private lessons, floor mats and suspension hard points, plus a public Quicket ticket flow and Instagram contact surface. Astrid's 2024 Shibari Study article confirms the space grew out of earlier South African rope-community organizing and had already hosted visiting educators such as Mango and Caritia.

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/communities/enbushinbakukai/

Fact — Enbushinbakukai (艶舞心縛會), 2000s–, is documented as a Japanese community in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 2000s–

Fact — Era: 2000s

Fact — Toyama-centered kinbaku community / teaching and photography project represented by Goku / 悟空. The current Bluesky profile characterizes Goku as representative of Enbushinbakukai, established for 17 years, with shows, events, private rope, lessons and kinbaku dojo activity in Toyama and Nagoya. MotionGallery's Kanna / Kagura project independently characterizes him as running a kinbaku bar at StudioG and regularly organizing kinbaku dojo, rope classes and Photo Nawa Club activity as Enbushinbakukai representative.

### Encordages Lémaniques

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/communities/encordages-lemaniques/

Fact — Encordages Lémaniques, 2010s–, is a Swiss community in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 2010s–

Fact — Era: 2010s

Fact — Lausanne-centered Swiss Romande / Vaud shibari and bondage community project. Its official site frames Encordages Lémaniques as a small-group rope-practice space between Lausanne and Geneva, with initiations, a three-step shibari course track, rope jams, rope picnics and invited workshops. The team page names Cordafortis / Markus and sweet_shiver / Christine as the current local pair: Markus says he started rope in 2015 and trains regularly, while Christine says Markus introduced her to rope in January 2017 and that they have since trained intensively via workshops and annual RopeRetreat participation. A 2026 current Markus / Cordafortis context / Cordafortis names Encordages Lémaniques as their linked Lausanne community / dojo established in 2017. An August 2025 official post records Marc Marukunawa as a guest teacher for two end-of-November workshops at Encordages Lémaniques.

### EscenaCR

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/communities/escenacr/

Fact — EscenaCR, 2013–, is a community in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 2013–

Fact — Era: 2010s

Fact — Costa Rica BDSM community active since 2013 with a durable San José-facing public infrastructure that explicitly includes rope/shibari programming. The official EscenaCR site frames the project as `Comunidad BDSM EscenaCR` with an online magazine, seminars, workshops, talks, munches, a MAsT San José chapter, Festival BDSM San José, Leather ECR and library resources; the same page also preserves direct rope traces such as `Taller Shibari progresivo 1 y 2`, `Show Shibari` and the named rope subgroup `Sinistra`. Bio.site and SoundCloud corroborate EscenaCR as an ongoing Costa Rica / Central America BDSM community focused on education, diffusion and support, while public contact surfaces point to @escenacr and the associated Kai / rope-education orbit.

Fact — Fesselkunstraum, 2020s–, is documented as a German community in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 2020s–

Fact — Era: 2020s

Fact — Strausberg / Ostbrandenburg shibari community project led by Andreas Regner, offering workshops, learning groups, jams, performances, private rope experiences and personalized training. Its current official site frames Fesselkunstraum around individual training, private sessions and performances, while the public Telegram profile characterizes Shibari in Strausberg with jams, picnics, performances, art installations and learning groups. Fesselkunstraum also has bounded Kinoko / Marie Sauvage event context: Shibari.Events records Maria Magdalena & @Fesselkunstraum as organizer of the 2025 CANK Berlin Hajime Kinoko show featuring Marie Sauvage, with further Kinoko-related workshop / organizer context preserved as reference-supported profile context. ### Hitchin' Bitches PDX

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/communities/hitchin-bitches-pdx/

Fact — Hitchin' Bitches PDX, 2018–, is documented as an American community in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 2018–

Fact — Era: 2010s

Fact — Portland chapter of Hitchin' Bitches International and a rope education community led by and for women and gender-diverse people. Its own About page characterizes HB PDX as an LGBTQIA / POC safer space for marginalized genders, intended to decenter cis-male, hetero and white narratives in rope and to give tops and bottoms a safer place to practice and enjoy rope for themselves. KinkFest 2026 names HitchinKitten as a former facilitator from 2018 to 2024.

### Hitchin' Bitches

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/communities/hitchin-bitches/

Fact — Hitchin' Bitches, 2011-, is a British community in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 2011-

Fact — Era: 2010s

Fact — London-established rope-learning and skill-sharing community for women / female-identified riggers and rope tops. Current Studio Kink wording says Hitchin' Bitches first started in late 2011 in London to provide a positive, relaxed supplementary environment in which more women could feel confident exploring rope bondage, while Hedwig's archived public bios corroborate her founding / co-organizing role in London from 2011. This entry covers the global origin rather than every later chapter; local chapters like Hitchin' Bitches PDX have separate records when their own public continuity is strong enough.

Fact — Hudson Valley / Kingston, New York rope-bondage community. Fact — Active: 2020s-

Fact — Miami rope / kinbaku community shell active in the mid-2020s. The current Dear Eleanor events page records recurring `Kinbaku Miami: Rope Jam` dates plus a `Kinbaku Miami: Application of Fundamentals` class in Miami, and the dedicated rope-jam pages describe a relaxed, community-led space where people of all levels can share, learn and explore shibari in a safe, supportive setting. Indy Rope Expo presenter material names Arkady The Sadist as founder of Kinbaku Miami, while Dear Eleanor preserves bounded guest-programming traces such as a May 2026 `Application of Fundamentals` class with Nevertheless and Nova.

### Kinkoi Shibari

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/communities/kinkoi-shibari/

Fact — Kinkoi Shibari, 2020s-, is a Spanish community in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 2020s-

Fact — Era: 2020s

Fact — Granada-centered non-profit sociocultural shibari / kinbaku association and self-managed rope community. Official pages frame Kinkoi as a space to learn, practice and build community around shibari, with progressive learning, care and consent, plus Peer to Peer, Jam, Sensory Lab, Rope Photo & Art and workshop formats. Its association page characterizes an LGTBIQ+ friendly, kink-positive and body-positive safer-space ethos, with roots in La Chana before a 2024 move to Zaidín.

Fact — Brittany BDSM association declared in 2018, with a public site describing Le Manoir as a non-commercial space for cooperation, exchange and convivial BDSM / D/s activities. Le Manoir organizes the recurring Breizh / Kinky Breizh Ropecamp between Nantes and Rennes, such as shibari spaces, rope / BDSM workshop categories, mini-munches and camping / lodging context.

### Oakland Rope Collective

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/communities/oakland-rope-collective/

Fact — Oakland Rope Collective, 2010s–, is documented as an American community in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 2010s–

Fact — Era: 2010s

Fact — Bay Area rope community oriented around traditionally inspired Japanese rope bondage and values-centered rope education. ShibariNews characterizes Oakland Rope Collective as a community focused on equity, accountability, accessibility, inclusion, compassion and comprehensive curriculum, with classes, workshops, events and play nights. Heartland Kinbaku's official team page provides the strongest documented connection by identifying SweetZephyr as a founder of Oakland Rope Collective in San Francisco, while the older standalone ORC domain no longer resolves in this review.

### Peer Rope Melbourne

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/communities/peer-rope-melbourne/

Fact — Peer Rope Melbourne, 2017–, is an Australian community in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 2017–

Fact — Era: 2010s

Fact — Melbourne shibari / kinbaku education community established by Avalon in 2017 after his 2015 move to Melbourne. Current official PRM pages frame the school as high-quality education and a safer, inclusive environment for kink and queer communities, with classes and workshops covering shibari, D/s dynamics, health and safety, and alternative relationship structures. Its 2026 Linktree and Humanitix host profile show continuing beginner-to-advanced in-person classes spanning north and south Melbourne, plus rope jams and workshops; the official teacher page names a current team such as Jess / StarLove, Barefoot Juliette, Sylb, CptFathom / Ashkrr and Magnus Rope.

### qMeet Shibari Study Group

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/communities/qmeet-amsterdam/

Fact — QMeet Shibari Study Group, 2020s-, is a Dutch community in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 2020s-

Fact — Era: 2020s

Fact — Amsterdam queer shibari study group organized via qMeet NL. The official qMeet shibari page characterizes a WhatsApp group and regular offline meetings, with free monthly practice meetings near Westerpark limited to 20 participants. The page frames the format as a study group rather than a jam: participants practice with different partners, share knowledge, ask questions, discuss, give feedback and set their own goals and boundaries. The public rules emphasize all-level participation, inclusion spanning gender / sexual orientation / ethnicity / age, and a safety frame of no nudity, no kissing and no sex.

Fact — Active: 2020s-

Fact — Era: 2020s

Fact — Rotterdam queer peer-rope community operating publicly as Queertied. Its WordPress page characterizes monthly peer rope for queer people and allies at Queer Gym Rotterdam, normally on the last Friday of the month, with stretching, peer rope and beginner / intermediate workshop blocks. Queer Gym's Peer Rope page corroborates the recurring last-Friday format and frames it as shibari / bondage peer practice for all levels, such as solo tying, partner practice, learning from books or videos, ground work and suspensions.

### Querverbindungen

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/communities/querverbindungen/

Fact — Querverbindungen, 2022-, is an Austrian community in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 2022-

Fact — Era: 2020s

Fact — Vienna rope-bondage community and `Kunst- und Kulturverein` focused on western and fusion rope bondage. The first-party site frames Querverbindungen as undogmatic, playful, arty and queer, with consent as a core value, a dogma-free peer-learning space, regular Monday bondage jams, Thursday munches, beginner onboarding and occasional guest-teacher events. The official past-events archive preserves recurring community activity from 2022 onward, such as dozens of annual bondage jams / munches plus hosted events with existing European rope teachers such as Andy Buru, Haag, Neya, RopuNawa, Misungui, Braxas and Michael Ropeknight; the 2026 upcoming-event list adds Sam Jay & Zero-Gi for an August special workshop weekend.

Fact — Active: 2011–

Fact — Era: 2010s

Fact — Pittsburgh rope-bondage education and practice community established by Russ / TwistedView in March 2011. Rope Bite PGH grew from a monthly class into open skillshares and practice, then shifted toward intensives and long-form education in 2018. Its current programme includes second-Sunday meetings, fundamentals, skillshares, rope jams and visiting national / international educators in safer and inclusive host spaces.

Fact — Rope Burn SF, 2020s–, is documented as an American community in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 2020s–

Fact — Era: 2020s

Fact — San Francisco Bay Area queer rope community established by BondageOtter / Vince. ShibariNews characterizes Rope Burn SF as a monthly men's rope jam and one of the largest Bay Area spaces for men interested in rope bondage, with a safe, sex-positive and skill-sharing focus. Rope Podcast and VoxBody describe the project as the USA's first ongoing masc-of-center, rope-specific play party plus skill share. SF Eagle's 2026 calendar also records Rope Burn SF presenting The Knotty Bird, a rope bondage social with suspension demos by pro riggers and Rope Burn instructors.

Fact — Rope Jam Barcelona, 2019–, is a Spanish community in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 2019–

Fact — Era: 2020s

Fact — Barcelona rope-bondage / shibari / kinbaku community hosting casual Sunday rope jams since at least 2019. The first-party site characterizes Rope Jam Barcelona as a group of rope enthusiasts who created a new space to meet other community members, practice and share, with feminist values, open welcome, and safety / respect as central principles. Public pages describe weekly Sunday 17:00-20:00 drop-in jams in a hired dance studio near Sant Antoni metro station, beginner-friendly attendance, communal jute rope, and an optional monthly supporting-membership model. Public Facebook group profile information repeats the same every-Sunday rope-jam framing and Barcelona location. The public trail emphasizes a recurring community jam in a hired venue rather than a permanent owned space.

### RUPPA

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/communities/ruppa-sicily/

Fact — RUPPA, 2020s–, is an Italian community in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 2020s–

Fact — Era: 2020s

Fact — South East Sicily shibari / rope community and nonprofit association. The official RUPPA site characterizes the group as riggers, rope bottoms and enthusiasts sharing a love of shibari, with public goals around spreading shibari practice, consent culture, BDSM information, inclusivity and LGBTQIA+ / queer-community themes. It locates the group in Catania, Modica and Siracusa, frames gallery traces for peer-rope and rope-bottoming events, and records a team such as Winter Violet / Serena, Bellerofonte / Ruben, Rock4by3 / Chiara, Silvaticus / Pietro, Kai, Warrior Pluto / Raffa, Sam and official photographer Gianluca Santanocito. The first-party site is used for RUPPA as a local rope-community entry only; it does not publicly corroborate a LuKkaS role, lineage, certification or formal school claim.

### Salzachknoten

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/communities/salzachknoten/

Fact — Salzachknoten, 2020s–, is an Austrian community in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 2020s–

Fact — Era: 2020s

Fact — Austrian shibari / rope-art association centered in Salzburg. The first-party site frames Salzachknoten as a `Verein` and a group of rope enthusiasts offering regular `Fesseltreff` evenings, beginner-friendly first-rope guidance, separate Rope Jam nights for experienced participants, and workshops focused on safe rope handling and the emotional / relational side of Japanese rope bondage. The association also organizes ShibaRetreat, an annual three-day Attergau programme combining impulse workshops, nature-centered tying, campfire practice and community retreat infrastructure. The public trail supports a durable association and programme relationship, not a single permanent venue or lineage school.

### Shibari Berlin

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/communities/shibari-berlin/

Fact — Shibari Berlin, 2016-, is documented as a German community in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 2016-

Fact — Era: 2010s

Fact — Berlin shibari / conscious-bondage workshop and community project. The first-party site characterizes Shibari Berlin as a group of friends who meet regularly to practice and learn from each other, organize workshops for all levels, and frame group sessions around safer, nonsexual space, consent, respect and safety. The project is also available for festivals, private sessions, photoshoots and performances. Its festival page says the idea grew from Fabian's Nowhere Festival encounter and small Berlin friend meetings; ShibariBerlin's first festival was Hedo Kongress in 2016, followed by Fusion, Wilde Moehre, Bucht der Traeumer, Feel, Artlake and other festival workshops.

The first-party site frames Shibari is the Way as Spokane's home for shibari classes, burlesque shows, Shibari and Flow Nights and nylon rope; its class page characterizes hands-on beginner via advanced rope training in Spokane, and its shows page frames the project with Spokane Flow Syndicate as a local collective producing burlesque, drag, flow-arts and rope-art shows. Fact — Active: 2024–

Fact — Era: 2020s

Safety / consent — Málaga rope community initiated by Martina Crepaz with Pol. Since 2024 it has combined progressive workshops with regular social jams and an explicit focus on consent, communication, safety and accessible participation in both tying and receiving roles. Its public programme has included foundations courses, photography workshops, semi-suspension study, social jams and a weekend intensive.

Fact — Active: 2023-

Fact — Era: 2020s

Fact — Budva-centered Montenegro shibari community / teaching project publicly operating as Shibari Montenegro / Shibari Kinbaku Montenegro. Its official Wix site frames the project around rope art in Budva and beginner via advanced shibari lessons / workshops, while the public Threads profile reports a friendly, clean and safe environment for shibari practice. The official About page characterizes an inclusive community created by two unnamed partners and projects such as Healing with Ropes and Rope Meditation. Shibari Montenegro hosted the four-day Ada Bojana Shibari Camp in September 2023; its main teacher and other named organizers remain programme context rather than person entries.

### Shibari Muses Collective

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/communities/shibari-muses/

Fact — Shibari Muses Collective, 2024–, is a community in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 2024–

Fact — Era: 2020s

Safety / consent — Puerto Rico-centered shibari collective established by Psyche and Eros. Its work combines rope with somatic awareness, embodiment, consent and trauma-informed safety practices. Public 2024 interviews describe experiences that may include poetry, meditation and song, with an emphasis on connection, self-exploration and care.

Fact — Era: 2020s

Fact — South Africa workshop and Rope Flow community platform led publicly by Iris-Moon. Quicket characterizes Shibari South Africa as a South Africa-wide shibari and rope-flow platform offering workshops and events around self-expression, creativity, connection, consent and mindfulness, with Cape Town and Johannesburg workshop traces. Cape Creative Collective and Rope Jam Studio independently identify Iris-Moon / Iris Moon as its founder in Cape Town event and London guest-workshop contexts.

### Shibari Träume

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/communities/shibari-traume/

Fact — Shibari Träume, 2021-, is a Greek community in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 2021-

Fact — Era: 2020s

Fact — Athens-centered queer shibari / rope-art collective active since at least 2021. Public Instagram and Facebook shells describe Shibari Träume as a local art group / collective offering rope-learning contact, collaborations and safety-minded practice. Independent Athens event traces place the group in cabaret-style performance programming and a 2023 `Shibari Canvas` life-drawing event with a semi-private Eriser Funem / VerminPaint performance. The public trail points to a recurring collective / event shell, not a dedicated permanent venue.

### Shibari Uruguay

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/communities/shibari-uruguay/

Fact — Shibari Uruguay, 2017–, is a community in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 2017–

Fact — Era: 2010s

Fact — Uruguay shibari community established in 2017 to promote shibari. Dercein's Latin American shibari resource characterizes @shibariuruguay as a non-profit organization that teaches classes, organizes workshops and open jams, and keeps studying with international teachers to disseminate rope practice in the region. A 2025 Dercein write-up credits Shibari Uruguay with coordinating and hosting the first-days-of-March Kirigami and Marta Tenshiko intensive workshop in Uruguay. The public Instagram profile frames the project as shibari dissemination in Uruguay, records @p0l.rope, @mery._kate and @uncle_fabi, and links a Facebook group for the same dissemination context.

### Shibari Valencia Venezuela

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/communities/shibari-valencia-ve/

Fact — Shibari Valencia Venezuela, 2023–, is a community in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 2023–

Fact — Era: 2020s

Fact — Valencia, Venezuela shibari community established in 2023 by Eysar Vargas. The current atlas context characterizes Shibari Valencia Venezuela as a woman-led, inclusive community dedicated to shibari as an artistic, erotic, physical and relational discipline, combining education, art, body research and community building. Its stated principles include informed consent, physical and emotional safety, prior negotiation, clear communication, aftercare, continuing education, professionalism, respect for the human body, inclusion, diversity and art as a language of expression. Activities include private lessons, introductory and continuing workshops, rope jams, practice sessions, life drawing with rope models, artistic photo shoots, performances, exhibitions, film screenings, community gatherings, rope picnics / rope club activity and interdisciplinary rope-yoga / body-well-being work. The community also reports selling practice supplies such as jute ropes, hanging rings, bamboo, carabiners and related items. The community programming includes beginner-accessible workshops, gatherings, artistic activities and observation spaces, and self-characterizes as the first Venezuelan space dedicated exclusively to shibari from erotic, artistic, educational and community perspectives. The group reports a permanent headquarters in Valencia, regular activity in Valencia and Maracay, and plans to expand programming to Caracas. Public-safe team context names Eysar Vargas as founder / director / educator / rigger, Juan Castillo as co-organizer / model / logistics coordinator, and Pau Bushi, Génesis Uquillas, Carlos Jairran and Fabiola Espiral as organizing support. Spanish is the primary language; English is functional for classes, events and international communication.

Fact — Active: 2022–

Fact — La Réunion shibari association centered in Saint-Denis. Public association registry data records ShibariPéï / Association de Shibari de La Réunion as an active loi 1901 association created in November 2022 to promote and develop shibari, l'art des cordes. ### Shibaru

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/communities/shibaru-community/

Fact — Shibaru, 2021–, is a Russian community in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 2021–

Fact — Era: 2020s

Fact — Moscow shibari / bondage community and recurring club-meeting format established by Nick Freerider. KinkAfisha's 2021 regular Shibaru meeting characterizes it as a friendly but rope-serious territory for information exchange, meeting known bondage artists, asking questions, free tying and beginner support, with eight suspension points, curators, a mini-seminar and Freerider & Zevsa in the show program. RopeFest Moscow 2023 names Nick Freerider as Shibaru founder, Zevsa as Shibaru organizer and Poryadnov as Shibaru co-organizer. RopeFest Peterburg 2026 names Yakov KINKKNIGHT as a Shibaru organizer since summer 2023, and a 2023 KINKKNIGHT article was published as part of the Shibaru project. A 2026 Por.Rope archive shows continued Shibaru meetings with open tying points, a performance, tea ceremony and social space; Nick Freerider's Telegram archive also preserves 2026 Shibaru meeting announcements with performances, free tying, more than ten suspension points, tea / social space and direct registration via Nikolay. Mamba's Nikita Ropekit expert bio names Ropekit as one of Shibaru's organizers. The larger Shibaru Fest format is covered separately as `shibaru-fest`.

Fact — Reykjavik rope / touch / bodywork community space created by Kata alongside Reykjavik Ropes. Shibari Study frames these Iceland spaces as emerging from Kata's rope, BDSM, spiritual and bodywork practice; Tamandua Kinbaku's 2021 schedule independently records Spektrum Reykjavik / Reykjavik Ropes as a public rope-workshop host.
